_meta.method: Manual extraction by reading the source document line-by-line and recording every quantitative measurement, dead-path reference, and synthesis-level finding present in the text. Anti-bias note: this annotation was done BEFORE running 70B extraction to avoid anchoring.

schema_1_hardware_measurement.property: RD bucket residency at 80 GB/s tier during 8B Q8 ANE inference
schema_1_hardware_measurement.measurement_method: tools/ioreport_sampler_v2 sampling PMP0 at 1Hz over 8 seconds

schema_1_hardware_measurement.property: RD active fraction during 8B Q8 ANE inference
RD active fraction during 70B GPU inference
RD active fraction at idle baseline

schema_1_hardware_measurement.measurement_method: Main 27 S5 geometry derivation, referenced as 'orders of magnitude smaller than working set' context
corpus reference to prior ANE bandwidth published figure

schema_1_hardware_measurement.property: peak measured upstream bandwidth ceiling for ANE
schema_1_hardware_measurement.measurement_method: tools/ioreport_sampler_v2 sampling PMP0, peak observed bucket excursion
schema_1_hardware_measurement.supersedes: 111 GB/s ANE channel ceiling figure from corpus

schema_2_dead_path.original_hypothesis: SLC tuning would deliver inference speedup via cache-hit-rate optimization on reused weights.
schema_2_dead_path.evidence_against: Working set (8B Q8 = 7.2 GB, 70B Q4 = 37 GB) is orders of magnitude larger than SLC (~48 MB), so each weight read is a cache miss that goes to DRAM. The 340x weight overwrite per token prevents any temporal reuse pattern.
schema_2_dead_path.scope: Dead as a HIT-RATE optimization. Working set won't fit, no temporal reuse opportunity exists.

schema_2_dead_path.path_name: SLC cache hints for any production workload
schema_2_dead_path.original_hypothesis: encodeCacheHintTag pinning would deliver bandwidth wins by keeping inference weights resident.
schema_2_dead_path.evidence_against: encodeCacheHintTag pins for hit-rate, which doesn't apply since the working set won't fit in SLC.
schema_2_dead_path.scope: Still dead. The Main 27 S3 K-reuse measurement and the unreachability of the API from ANE/CPU paths together confirm no production path benefits.

schema_3_architectural_finding.finding: The SLC is a high-bandwidth streaming gateway, not a working-set cache.
schema_3_architectural_finding.detail: Every DRAM byte transits through the AMCC controller during inference. The cache hit rate is near zero (working set ≫ SLC capacity), but the AMCC controller sustains 80-160 GB/s read bandwidth and is the actual upstream bandwidth ceiling for ANE workloads — higher than the published 111 GB/s ANE channel figure.

evidence_chain.measurement: Cacheability analysis of inference working sets
evidence_chain.result: Weights overwrite SLC ~340x per token; no temporal reuse possible at the working-set scale

evidence_chain.measurement: PMP0 AMCC RD bucket residency during 8B Q8 ANE inference (tools/ioreport_sampler_v2, 1Hz × 8s)
evidence_chain.result: 80 GB/s bucket held for 67.97% of wall clock; excursions to 160 GB/s; 94.03% active overall

evidence_chain.measurement: PMP0 AMCC RD bucket residency during 70B GPU inference

implications: The SLC dead-path entry must distinguish CACHE HIT RATE (still ~0%) from BANDWIDTH GATEWAY (sustained 80-160 GB/s, load-bearing)
The published 111 GB/s ANE channel ceiling may be incomplete — the AMCC layer measured 160 GB/s peak
Future 'SLC is irrelevant to inference' claims must reference this finding to avoid the cacheability vs bandwidth conflation
SLC tuning experiments should not be proposed without re-checking whether the AMCC bandwidth ceiling is the actual constraint
contradicts: The Main 27 conclusion 'SLC dead for inference' as a universal claim — it is now sharpened to 'dead as a hit-rate optimization' only
